auto_posts.title: Azure Local Networking Architecture: Planning for Scale and Performance
auto_posts.subtitle: Guidance on software-defined networking, NIC bonding, and cluster connectivity best practices updated
auto_posts.content:
Azure Local deployments require careful planning around networking architecture—particularly when scaling beyond initial proof-of-concept environments. Recent Azure documentation updates address common questions around NIC bonding strategies, RDMA configuration, and multi-site connectivity patterns.

Key Updates:

  • NIC Bonding Best Practices: Guidance updated to reflect real-world deployments. For most production environments, traditional NIC teaming (LACP) provides adequate redundancy and performance. RDMA-capable deployments require separate, non-bonded NICs to preserve direct access to storage traffic.
  • Cluster Connectivity Models: Azure Local documentation now explicitly distinguishes between single-site, multi-site, and hybrid connectivity patterns. Single-site clusters (all nodes in one rack or room) support simplified configurations. Multi-site scenarios require careful latency management—especially for stretched clusters running 23H2, which must migrate to non-stretched 24H2 architectures by April 2026.
  • Network Performance Validation: New sections on measuring and validating network throughput and latency using built-in Azure Local tools. These diagnostics help identify bandwidth bottlenecks before they impact production workloads.
  • Software-Defined Networking (SDN) Enablement: For customers leveraging Azure Local's SDN capabilities, the updated documentation clarifies how network adapters are reserved for compute vs. management plane usage, and how this affects overall node density calculations.

    • The underlying principle remains consistent: Azure Local networking should be designed with redundancy and future growth in mind. A typical three-node or four-node cluster benefits from dedicated management, storage, and VM traffic lanes—either through physical separation or intelligent VLAN tagging.

    For detailed guidance, refer to the updated Azure Local planning documentation on networking and cluster design.
